summary: McKenzie documents his migration from WordPress.com to a self-hosted WordPress installation after two years of planning. He provides a step-by-step technical guide covering domain registration, DNS changes, content export/import, and post-migration setup. The move was motivated by wanting full control over plugins, themes, and site customization as his business expanded beyond just blogging about Bingo Card Creator. He notes practical details like the importance of maintaining WordPress.com's 301 redirects for SEO continuity and the immediate spam problem that hits self-hosted blogs.
key_points: Maintaining WordPress.com's $10/year domain redirect is essential for preserving SEO value during migration via 301 redirects
Self-hosting gives control over plugins, themes, and site direction — important as a business outgrows a simple blog
The migration process is straightforward but requires patience, particularly waiting for Google to recognize the domain change
Self-hosted WordPress blogs get hammered by comment spam immediately — Akismet is a necessity, not an option
money_quotes: The main reasons is to make me the captain of the blog's destiny, as opposed to WordPress.
WordPress will 301 redirect to your new domain IF AND ONLY IF you continue paying them the $10 a year — its a steal, do it.
I got 50 lovely comments welcoming me to my new digs within the first 15 minutes. They all seemed to be by people who work in the pharmaceutical industry.
key_insight: When migrating a blog with established SEO value, preserving link equity through proper 301 redirects is worth far more than the nominal cost.
